flashcards.front: What is an Entity in ER modeling?
flashcards.back: An entity is a real-world object that can be uniquely identified and exists independently. Examples: Student, Employee, Product. It is represented as a rectangle in ER diagrams.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Entity Set and Entity Type?
flashcards.back: Entity Type is the abstract definition of a group of entities (e.g., STUDENT). Entity Set is the actual collection of entities of a particular type at a given time.

flashcards.front: Define Composite Attribute with an example.
flashcards.back: An attribute that can be divided into smaller sub-attributes. Example: Address can be broken into Street, City, State, Pincode. Represented as ellipses connected to each other.
An attribute that can hold multiple values for a single entity. Example: PhoneNumber (a person can have multiple phones). Represented as double ellipse in ER diagram.
An attribute whose value can be computed from other attributes. Example: Age can be derived from DateOfBirth. Represented as dashed ellipse in ER diagrams.
A minimal set of attributes that uniquely identifies each entity in an entity set. It cannot be null and must have unique values. Example: StudentID in STUDENT entity.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Candidate Key and Primary Key?
flashcards.back: Candidate Key is any attribute that can uniquely identify entities (there can be multiple). Primary Key is the selected candidate key used to identify entities in the table.

flashcards.front: Define a Weak Entity with example.
flashcards.back: An entity that cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone and depends on a strong entity. Example: Dependent entity depends on Employee (has no own key, uses EmployeeID + DependentName).

flashcards.front: What is Total Participation in a relationship?
flashcards.back: Every entity in an entity set must participate in at least one relationship. Denoted by double line in ER diagram. Example: Every student must enroll in a course.

flashcards.front: What is Partial Participation in a relationship?
flashcards.back: An entity may or may not participate in a relationship. Denoted by single line. Example: An employee may or may not manage a department.
Cardinality specifies the number of instances of one entity that can relate to a single instance of another entity. Types: 1:1, 1:N, M:N.
A relationship involving three entity sets. Example: Project-Employee-Skill relationship where an employee uses a skill on a project. Represented as a diamond connected to three entities.
A relationship where an entity relates to itself. Example: Employee manages Employee (supervisor-subordinate). Entity participates twice with different roles.
An attribute in one table that refers to the Primary Key of another table. Used to establish relationships between tables. Example: DeptID in Employee table referencing DeptID in Department table.
A visual representation of the database schema showing entities, attributes, and relationships. Developed by Peter Chen in 1976 using Chen notation (rectangles, ellipses, diamonds).
